assumptions: Task description was provided and only used as a low-risk planning hint.
Spec text was provided and only used where it did not contradict the contract.

expected_properties: Observed outputs or protocol-visible signals settle to a stable, non-X post-reset state.
No fixed-cycle completion is assumed during reset recovery.
cases.goal: Establish a stable post-reset baseline before functional checking.
notes: Reset polarity may still be heuristic if the contract marks it ambiguous.
preconditions: Reset signal is available in the contract.
stimulus_intent: Assert the detected reset using the inferred polarity.
Release reset conservatively and observe interface stabilization.
timing_assumptions: Do not assume completion before it becomes externally visible.

expected_properties: Externally visible outputs remain defined and can change across conservative clocked observation windows.
The plan observes progress without assuming a fixed-cycle protocol or hidden internal state access.
After observing 60 consecutive clock edges where Secs wraps from 59 to 0, Mins should increment by 1 while Hours remains unchanged.
Secs should cycle through values 0 to 59 repeatedly without X or Z states.
cases.goal: Validate correct second counting and minute rollover behavior under conservative clock observation.
notes: Case intent is conservative when the contract is weak or timing is unresolved.
Case relies on deterministic clock-driven observation because no non-control inputs were resolved.
No assumption about exact latency; only relies on observable output changes across clock edges.
preconditions: Use only ports and observable signals present in the contract.
stimulus_intent: Release reset if present and observe a small number of conservative clock edges for externally visible progress.
Allow the design to run freely after reset and monitor natural progression of time counters.
timing_assumptions: Do not assume completion before it becomes externally visible.
Observe at least 120 clock cycles to capture multiple second and minute transitions.

expected_properties: When Hours=23, Mins=59, Secs=59, the next rising edge of CLK should produce Hours=0, Mins=0, Secs=0.
cases.goal: Confirm correct wrap‑around of the calendar at the 24‑hour boundary.
notes: The case relies on reaching the boundary condition through natural progression; no forced stimulus other than the clock is required.
Case relies on deterministic clock-driven observation because no non-control inputs were resolved.
preconditions: Module is out of reset and has progressed to the state Hours=23, Mins=59, Secs=59.
stimulus_intent: Observe the behavior of the design at the day rollover point.
timing_assumptions: One clock edge after the boundary condition is sufficient to verify the wrap‑around.

unresolved_items: Spec/reset hint could not be mapped to a known reset port: The calendar module uses three always blocks to update the values of seconds, minutes, and hours based on the clock signal and reset signal.

Spec/reset hint could not be mapped to a known reset port: The second always block also triggers on the positive edge of the clock signal or the positive edge of the reset signal. It handles the minutes value (Mins). If the reset signal is active, it sets the minutes value to 0. If both the minutes and seconds values are 59, it wraps around and sets the minutes value to 0. If the seconds value is 59, it increments the minutes value by 1. Otherwise, it keeps the minutes value unchanged.

Spec/reset hint could not be mapped to a known reset port: The third always block triggers on the positive edge of the clock signal or the positive edge of the reset signal. It handles the hours value (Hours). If the reset signal is active, it sets the hours value to 0. If the hours, minutes, and seconds values are all at their maximum (23, 59, and 59 respectively), it wraps around and sets the hours value to 0. If the minutes and seconds values are both 59, it increments the hours value by 1. Otherwise, it keeps the hours value unchanged.
Contract strength is limited; generated cases favor safe observation over precise timing assumptions.
